# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Central Europe Climate NGO, Policy Vote

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5+ concepts across archetypes)

All 8 brief fields are present. This is Mode A — full-brief mode. No assumptions table needed.

I am describing the opposition at the category level because the brief did not name specific organizations — it identified them as "industry-front groups" and "fossil-fuel-lobby-linked opposition." That level of abstraction is maintained throughout.

---

### Concept A — "Follow the Money to the Vote"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative  
**One-line thesis:** The opposition is running "affordable energy" and "energy sovereignty" frames funded by industry-front groups — your job is not to out-argue those frames but to make the funding itself the story, forcing journalists and citizens to ask who benefits from the policy vote, before they debate the policy itself.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 + Tier 2 (earned media lead; volunteer networks amplify)  
**Authenticity hook:** The Direkt36-tier investigative journalism partner. A co-published funding trail — who funds the industry-front groups, what they've donated to, what the policy vote is worth to them — is the kind of thing your opposition cannot credibly refute because the documents are public. This is a real action, not a narrative trick.  
**Minimum viable version:** In two weeks: work with the journalism partner to identify and verify one specific financial link between the opposition campaign and a fossil-fuel-linked entity. Publish as a co-branded investigative short-form piece. Seed to your 8,000 email list and let earned media take it from there.

---

### Concept B — "Verified Voices"
**Archetype:** Community-Build + Coalition Play  
**One-line thesis:** Your trust bottleneck is greenwashing fatigue — audiences believe the problem but not the messengers. The fix is not better NGO comms; it's moving the messenger. A curated network of trusted non-NGO civic voices (doctors, teachers, parents, local mayors, farmers affected by climate events) vouching for the petition gives the campaign a trust substrate that no amount of polished ad creative can manufacture.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (volunteer networks, local chapter meetups already running in Budapest and Krakow, peer-to-peer sharing)  
**Authenticity hook:** Real people with non-NGO credibility making the ask in their own words, on their own accounts. The campaign provides the landing page and the ask; the voices provide the trust. A rural Polish farmer who lost crops to flooding is not an NGO spokesperson and cannot be attacked as one.  
**Minimum viable version:** In two weeks: identify 20 non-NGO civic voices in Hungary, Poland, Czechia, and Slovakia who are willing to share a one-paragraph personal statement. Publish 5 of them, one per weekday across the NGO's Instagram and email list. Measure shares and sign-up conversions per voice.

---

### Concept C — "The Cost, In Their Words"
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc / Named Beneficiary Earned Media  
**One-line thesis:** The opposition owns abstract economic frames ("affordable energy," "energy sovereignty"). You cannot win on their terrain. Shift terrain: make the cost of inaction specific, local, and personal — a named family in Krakow, a named town in Hungary, a named professional whose livelihood is already changing — told in their words, not the NGO's.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (email list, volunteer networks to identify and recruit real subjects) + Tier 2 (earned media; the journalism partnership makes this credible and scalable)  
**Authenticity hook:** A real named person experiencing a real, verifiable climate-linked cost in the target region. Not statistics. Not projections. A 45-year-old teacher in Miskolc who can't insure her house against flooding anymore. That is not deniable.  
**Minimum viable version:** In two weeks: recruit one named subject through the volunteer coordinator's existing regional network. Record a 3-minute phone or in-person video of them describing the cost in their own words. Publish with the journalism partner's co-brand. No polished production — phone footage is the format.

---

### Concept D — "5,000 Names Before the Vote"
**Archetype:** Movement Mobilization / Fundraising Drive (hybrid)  
**One-line thesis:** The policy vote has a hard date. The 5,000-supporter goal maps directly onto a visible countdown. A public-facing commitment tracker, updated daily, turns every new donor and petition signer into proof of momentum — and momentum is the one thing paid opposition advertising cannot fake. The campaign becomes the evidence that the opposition isn't speaking for everyone.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (email list is the engine; volunteer networks drive in-person signups at Budapest and Krakow meetups) + Tier 2 (organic social sharing of milestone moments)  
**Authenticity hook:** The real number, updated in real time, displayed publicly. No inflated claims, no "over 4,000 supporters" when it's 3,800. The counter's credibility is its accuracy. When it reaches 5,000, the press release writes itself.  
**Minimum viable version:** In two weeks: build a simple public-facing counter (can be a daily post, a landing page, or a basic tracker). Send one email to the 8,000-person list with the current count and a direct ask. Measure how many of the ~960 expected openers (12% open rate) convert within 72 hours.

---

### Concept E — "Counter-Program"
**Archetype:** Earned-Media Stunt + Coalition Play  
**One-line thesis:** The opposition is running slick paid Facebook campaigns — "affordable energy," "energy sovereignty." Those frames are designed for an audience that never hears the other side. Your move: organize a one-day, in-person counter-program event in a high-visibility Central European location timed to coincide with the most newsworthy moment in the policy calendar, with a joint declaration from 5+ allied organizations. Not a protest — a credentialed alternative policy moment that earns press coverage the ads cannot buy.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (earned media lead) + Tier 1 (volunteer logistics, coalition partners provide amplification to their own lists)  
**Authenticity hook:** A signed joint declaration with named partner organizations is a real artifact. It's not a claim about what you believe — it's a document that exists. The journalism partner's coverage gives it third-party credibility. The event, even if 200 people attend, is covered as an institutional moment, not as an NGO press release.  
**Minimum viable version:** In two weeks: identify 3 coalition partner organizations willing to co-sign a joint statement. Draft the statement. Set the date 6 weeks out. This is the week-1 and week-2 action; the event itself comes later.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**This plan is written for Concept A ("Follow the Money to the Vote") as the primary campaign spine, with Concept D ("5,000 Names Before the Vote") as the mobilization layer running in parallel.**

The logic: your trust bottleneck is greenwashing skepticism — audiences believe the problem, not NGO messengers. Counter-narrative (Concept A) directly addresses the messenger problem by making the opposition's credibility the story rather than re-litigating yours. Concept D gives the counter-narrative a concrete action to convert to: sign the petition, donate, become one of the 5,000. These two concepts reinforce each other rather than competing for attention.

The Direkt36-tier journalism partnership is the irreplaceable asset that makes Concept A work. Without it, Concept A is just an NGO making allegations. With it, it is a credentialed investigation.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Your asymmetry is severe (opponent spends approximately 30× what you spend).** This means the organic Tier 1–2 stack carries the campaign, paid serves only as surgical amplification of proven organic winners, and a geo-holdout lift test runs before any broad Facebook/Instagram spend is approved.

The board's instinct to allocate 40% (approximately €4,800) to Facebook/Instagram ads should be held until the lift test in Section 10 returns a result — the prior €4k run with poor conversions is a pre-signal that the paid channel is not solving a trust problem and may be making it worse.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

**Score: 3/3 — MMF confirmed. This is a distribution problem, not a demand problem.**

1. **Revenue/commitment signal (YES):** The NGO has 8,000 email subscribers and a functioning volunteer coordinator running active regional meetups. These are real committed people, not passive impressions.

2. **Language signal (YES):** The brief gives us the specific language the opposition uses ("affordable energy," "energy sovereignty") and names the pain the audience articulates: the belief gap — "they believe the problem but not the messengers." That is a sentence from real experience, not an inferred concern.

3. **Close signal (YES):** A warm email list at 12% open rates, an established journalism partnership, and active regional meetup infrastructure all indicate that a direct ask to commit (sign, donate) would convert at meaningfully above zero.

MMF is confirmed. Proceed to Stage 4.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Trust compounding.** The bottleneck is not that the audience can't find the NGO. It's that they find it and don't trust it enough to act. Every channel choice is subordinated to this function.

**Sector rider applied:** Rider 3 (Nonprofit / NGO / Cause-Led). Primary channel fit: Tier 1 volunteer networks + owned email list + Tier 2 earned media anchored to named stories and accountability moments. Paid reserved for warm-audience retargeting only — never cold acquisition.

The rider also surfaces the sector's failure mode: **donor-speak instead of story.** Every piece of content is checked against one rule: does it lead with a named person and a specific moment, or does it lead with organizational outputs and policy language? If the latter, rewrite before publishing.

---

**Prioritized channel stack:**

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Primary function | Weekly effort |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Investigative journalism partnership (Direkt36-tier) | Tier 2 | Trust compounding — third-party credibility | 5h/week (liaison, content review, source access) |
| 2 | Email newsletter to 8,000-subscriber list | Tier 1 | Trust compounding — owned, direct, platform-proof | 4h/week (1 send/week, segmented by engagement) |
| 3 | Volunteer coordinator + regional meetup network | Tier 1 | Mobilization — peer-to-peer ask converts at rates ads cannot match | 6h/week (coordinator + content for meetup use) |
| 4 | Organic Instagram (2,400 followers) | Tier 2 | Trust compounding — visual storytelling, named subjects | 3h/week (3 posts/week, human subjects, unpolished) |
| 5 | Organic Facebook Groups (not Page) | Tier 2 | Trust compounding — regional communities in HU/PL/CZ/SK | 3h/week (active moderation + posting in relevant groups) |
| 6 | Coalition partner channels | Tier 1/2 | Reach via borrowed credibility — partner newsletter swaps, co-posts | 2h/week (outreach + coordination) |
| 7 | Warm Facebook/Instagram retargeting | Tier 3 | Paid amplification — ONLY after organic traction gate passed, ONLY to warm audiences (email list + past engagers) | 2h/week (monitoring + creative refreshes; 24–48h gate applies) |

**Total weekly effort: 25h/week.** With 3 FTE campaigners + 2 comms staff at 50%, the team's effective weekly capacity on this campaign is approximately 120h/week total. The 25h/week channel stack uses 21% of available capacity, leaving substantial room for relationship management, press outreach, and the first-30-days actions in Section 8.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Allocation: 85% organic / 15% paid.**

Rationale: Severe asymmetry (1:30 spend ratio) combined with a trust bottleneck and a prior failed paid run (€4k in, poor conversions) makes an 80/20 split the floor. The specific trust problem here — greenwashing skepticism — means paid reach actively works against the campaign's core goal. An ad on Meta from an NGO is exactly the format the target audience has learned to distrust. The 15% paid allocation is reserved entirely for warm retargeting (email list custom audiences, website visitor retargeting) after organic content has demonstrated traction.

**In euros:**
- Total budget: €12,000 over 4 months (~€3,000/month)
- Organic execution (research, design, event logistics, volunteer tools): ~€10,200 (85%)
- Paid (warm retargeting only, post-traction-gate): ~€1,800 (15%)

The board's current 40% (€4,800) allocation to Facebook/Instagram ads is refused at this stage. The rationale is presented plainly: the prior €4k paid run converted poorly, the trust bottleneck is not a reach problem, and broad Facebook ads from an NGO in a greenwashing-skeptic market will deepen the credibility gap rather than close it. If the geo-holdout lift test in Section 10 shows positive incremental lift from warm retargeting, the board can revisit allocation at the 8-week mark with data. Without that test, increasing paid spend on a channel that already underperformed would be spending into a confirmed failure mode.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

The brief does not name specific opposition organizations. I am describing them at the category level — "industry-front groups" and "fossil-fuel-lobby-linked opposition" — as the brief provided. Do not fill in organization names.

---

### What the opposition saturates

**Industry-front groups and fossil-fuel-lobby-linked opposition are flooding:**
- Paid Facebook and Instagram campaigns with slick, agency-produced creative
- "Affordable energy" and "energy sovereignty" message frames — economic-anxiety-led, not environmental
- Paid influencer relationships with 4 named mid-tier creators (per brief; their names are not provided, and the skill does not invent them)
- High-production video formats emphasizing visual polish and professional spokespeople
- Emotionally negative framing: fear of energy poverty, fear of economic disruption, fear of external control
- Broad cold-audience targeting across Central European markets

The format itself signals "this is funded." High-production paid social from an industry-linked organization in a greenwashing-skepticism context is already suspect before a frame is read.

---

### The absence that becomes your signal

**Your refusal to produce slick paid ads is the message.**

The opposition's saturation is paid, polished, and industry-funded. Your presence is investigation-backed, human-voiced, and volunteer-powered. That contrast is not a liability to be apologized for — it is the core positioning signal. A campaign that does not look like theirs, by design, is the campaign that gets believed.

Specifically:
- Opposition runs paid Facebook creative → your absence from paid Facebook, combined with the journalism partner co-brand, signals "we don't buy attention, we earn it."
- Opposition uses polished influencer content → your use of non-NGO civic voices (farmers, teachers, doctors) in their own words from their own locations signals "we don't need to pay people to say this."
- Opposition leads with economic frame ("affordable energy") → your counter leads with accountability frame ("who funds this claim, and what do they stand to gain from the vote?")

This is not aggression; it is differentiation. You do not match their register. You do not mirror their format. You operate on a different axis entirely.

---

### One-sentence positioning line

**"We don't buy your attention — we bring you the documents."**

This line commits the campaign to: (1) organic and earned-media channels over paid, (2) document-backed accountability journalism over advocacy assertions, (3) third-party credibility (journalism partner) over NGO self-promotion. Every piece of content must be checkable against this line. If a piece of content looks like an ad, it violates the line. If a piece of content makes a claim without a named source or document, it violates the line.

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape 1 — Earned-Media First (recommended primary shape)

**How it works:** The Direkt36-tier journalism partnership is the engine. Every 2–3 weeks, a new co-published investigative short — one specific funding link, one specific document, one specific accountability question — is released. The email list gets early access. Volunteers share it in regional Facebook Groups. The NGO's Instagram covers the reaction (supporters, civic responses, policy responses). The petition/donation ask is attached to each release as the action.

**Self / Us / Now flagship content:**
- *Self:* A campaign coordinator speaking directly to camera from their desk: "I joined this organization five years ago because I believed the problem was real. What I didn't expect was spending this year figuring out who is paying to make you think it isn't."
- *Us:* "Every family in Hungary, Poland, Czechia, and Slovakia is going to live with the outcome of this vote. The people funding the 'affordable energy' campaign will not."
- *Now:* "The vote is in four months. We're publishing what we found. We need 5,000 people to tell the committee they're watching. Sign here before [date]."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Strongest on the trust problem: journalism-backed content is the format that greenwashing skeptics trust most.
- Depends heavily on the journalism partnership delivering on time and on quality. If the partner's bandwidth is limited, the cadence breaks.
- Slower to scale than paid channels. The first release may reach 5,000–15,000 people organically; virality is not guaranteed.
- Most durable: each piece remains indexed, citable, and shareable long after the vote.

---

### Shape 2 — Community-First (builds the trust base before making the ask)

**How it works:** Before any public content push, the volunteer coordinator recruits 50–100 "Verified Voices" — non-NGO civic contacts (doctors, teachers, local elected officials, farmers) in each target country — and gives each a one-paragraph personal statement and a petition link. These voices share from their own accounts, in their own language, to their own networks. The NGO's role is invisible: it provides the landing page, the petition infrastructure, and the coordination. The voices provide the trust.

**Self / Us / Now flagship content:**
- *Self:* A non-NGO voice — e.g., a Krakow school principal — recording a 90-second phone video: "I'm not an activist. I'm not paid to say this. But my students' parents are asking me about the vote, and I thought someone should explain what it actually means."
- *Us:* "None of us signed up to follow a policy vote. But this vote is going to affect whether the region our kids grow up in looks the way we remember it."
- *Now:* "I've signed. If you trust me enough to read this far, here's the link."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Highest trust signal, because the NGO is not the messenger.
- Slowest to execute. Recruiting and briefing 50–100 credible non-NGO voices takes 3–4 weeks minimum. This compresses the 4-month window significantly.
- Hard to control quality. Some voices will go off-message; some will not follow through.
- Best conversion rate per share: peer-to-peer asks from trusted contacts convert at 3–5× the rate of brand content.

---

### Shape 3 — Mobilization Countdown (urgency-first, policy-vote deadline as the spine)

**How it works:** The public-facing counter — "X / 5,000 names before the vote" — is the spine. Every piece of content references the current count. The email list gets weekly updates. Each regional meetup is a in-person sign-up event. Coalition partners run co-branded drives with their own lists. The journalism partnership publishes one major accountability piece at the campaign's midpoint (week 8) to generate a surge. The countdown creates organic urgency the opposition's paid saturation cannot manufacture.

**Self / Us / Now flagship content:**
- *Self:* A campaign coordinator writing an email to the list: "When we started this, I told our board we'd get 5,000 names. They looked skeptical. So did I, honestly. We're at [current count]. Here's what I've seen change since we started."
- *Us:* "Every name on this list is a person who read the documents, checked the sources, and still signed. That's what makes this count matter."
- *Now:* "We're [X] names from the goal. The vote is [Y] weeks away. If you haven't signed, the link is here. If you have, forward this to one person you trust."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Creates real urgency and visible momentum — the counter itself is news when it hits milestones.
- Depends on early momentum. A slow start makes the goal feel impossible; the tracker needs to show movement within the first 2 weeks.
- Simplest to execute operationally. No journalism dependency for early weeks; no massive volunteer recruitment sprint.
- Weakest on the trust problem in isolation — a countdown doesn't address why audiences should trust the NGO. Needs the journalism partnership piece (Shape 1) at the midpoint to close the trust gap.

**Recommendation:** Run Shape 1 (Earned-Media First) as the primary spine, with Shape 3's countdown mechanic (the public 5,000-name tracker) layered on as the conversion infrastructure. Shape 2's community-of-voices approach is the week-1–2 recruitment task feeding into Shape 1's subsequent releases. These three shapes are not mutually exclusive — they are sequenced layers.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Capacity ceiling:** 120h/week total team. The plan below uses approximately 40–50h/week across weeks 1–4 — well within capacity. The remaining bandwidth is for unplanned relationship management, press responses, and coordination overhead.

**Shape: Earned-Media First with Mobilization Countdown layer**

---

### Week 1 — Foundation

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brief the journalism partner on Concept A scope; agree on first investigation focus and publication timeline | Campaign lead + one comms staff | 6h | Written scope agreement with publication date by Friday |
| Build the public-facing "5,000 names" tracker (simple landing page or daily Instagram post cadence — no elaborate build needed) | One comms staff | 4h | Tracker live and linked from all existing NGO profiles by Thursday |
| Send email to full 8,000-person list: explain the campaign, the vote date, the 5,000-name goal, and link to the petition | Campaign lead draft, comms review | 3h | Email sent by Wednesday; track unique clicks and conversions over 72h |
| Begin recruiting 20 "Verified Voices" — non-NGO civic contacts in target countries — via volunteer coordinator's regional networks | Volunteer coordinator | 8h | 10 confirmed voices with contact and biography by Friday |
| Research: identify the top 5 climate and policy-adjacent newsletters, podcasts, and journalists across HU/PL/CZ/SK for earned-media outreach — produce a ranked list with contact channels | One campaign staffer | 4h | Ranked list of 15+ targets with contact channel by end of week |
| Publish 2 Instagram posts: one named-person story (sourced from meetup network), one counter-framing the "affordable energy" message with a specific documented fact | Comms staff | 3h | Track saves and shares per post over 48h; shares > likes is the signal |

**Week 1 total effort: ~28h**

---

### Week 2 — First content release and list activation

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Co-publish first investigative short with journalism partner: one specific documented funding link, framed as "who funds the 'affordable energy' claim and what's the vote worth to them?" | Campaign lead + journalism partner | 5h (NGO side) | Article published; NGO sends email to list within 24h of publication; 3+ shares by other journalists or policy accounts |
| Activate 5 "Verified Voices": each publishes a personal statement on their own account with petition link | Volunteer coordinator | 4h | 5 posts live by Wednesday; track petition sign-ups attributable to each voice via UTM tags |
| Pitch first wave of earned-media targets from Week 1 research list | Campaign staffer | 4h | Personalized pitch sent to top 8 targets by Tuesday; success = reply or mention within 5 days |
| Publish 3 Instagram posts: 2 named-subject stories + 1 milestone update ("We're at [X] names — here's who's signing and why") | Comms staff | 3h | Milestone post generates shares from at least 3 accounts not in existing follower base |
| Send second email to list: share the investigation piece, current counter, and one personal story from a "Verified Voice" | Campaign lead | 2h | Track open rate vs. first send; track conversion rate from email click to petition sign |

**Week 2 total effort: ~18h**

---

### Week 3 — Coalition activation and Facebook Groups

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reach out to 3 coalition partner organizations for cross-promotion: newsletter swap, co-post, or joint statement (for Concept E if pursued) | Campaign lead | 4h | At least 2 confirmed partnerships with timeline by Friday |
| Begin systematic posting in target Facebook Groups in HU, PL, CZ, SK — regional environment/policy/civic groups. Use named-person stories, not NGO announcements. | Campaign staffer | 5h | Active in 8+ relevant groups by end of week; track join requests or engagement in each group |
| Second "Verified Voices" wave: activate 10 more voices, including at least 3 in rural areas outside Budapest and Krakow | Volunteer coordinator | 5h | 10 posts live with UTM-tracked links; review conversion rate per voice category (professional vs. community figure) |
| Publish second journalism partner short — pivot to the "cost in their words" angle: one named regional subject | Campaign lead + journalism partner | 4h (NGO side) | Published; at least 1 pickup by another outlet or journalist |
| UTM attribution review: rank all channels by conversion rate per unique visitor (email, Instagram, journalist referrals, Facebook Groups, Verified Voices) | Comms staff | 2h | Ranked list; identify top and bottom channel; make Week 4 reallocation decision |

**Week 3 total effort: ~20h**

---

### Week 4 — Optimization and warm retargeting gate

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Review traction gate for all published content: any Instagram post with strong save/share ratio in 48h qualifies for warm retargeting boost | Comms staff | 1h | Identify top 1–2 qualifying posts |
| If traction gate passed: launch warm Facebook/Instagram retargeting ad (email list custom audience only; budget €450 for the month; maximum frequency cap: 5 impressions per person per 2-week period) | Comms staff | 3h | Track petition sign-ups with UTM "paid-retarget" source; compare cost-per-sign-up vs. organic channels |
| Budapest and Krakow meetup: in-person sign-up event at existing regional meetup, timed to align with Week 4 | Volunteer coordinator | 6h | Minimum 50 in-person sign-ups across both locations |
| Send third email: midpoint update — current counter, stories from the Verified Voices, specific ask ("We're halfway to the vote — here's what we need this week") | Campaign lead | 2h | Track conversion rate; compare to Weeks 1 and 2 baselines |
| Assess: has the traction gate been passed by any content? If yes, brief board on data before any paid budget increase. If no, redirect the €4,800 board-allocated paid budget to coalition and journalism partnership costs | Campaign lead | 2h | Written decision memo to board by Friday |

**Week 4 total effort: ~14h**

---

**Earned-media action specifics (per SKILL.md Stage 5 requirement):**

The Week 1 research sprint produces a ranked list of named targets. Until that list exists, I cannot name specific podcasts or newsletters in the Central European climate/policy space without inventing them — and the skill's anti-fabrication rule prohibits that. Assigning target research as a Week 1 action is the correct move.

Success criteria for the research sprint: a ranked list of 15 targets with: (a) outlet name, (b) contact channel (email or LinkedIn), (c) recent story or episode that shows the hook match, and (d) language (HU/PL/CZ/SK/EN).

For the Week 2 pitch wave: personalized pitches sent to the top 8 targets, matching hook to recent content. Success = reply or mention within 5 days. If no replies by end of Week 2, pivot outreach strategy and brief on why (wrong hook, wrong channel, wrong target tier).

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Scope:** Paid applies only as warm retargeting of email list + past website visitors. Broad cold-paid (Facebook/Instagram cold audiences) is refused at severe asymmetry with a trust bottleneck. The prior €4k paid run with poor conversions is consistent with what the research predicts: paid ads from NGOs into greenwashing-skeptic audiences make the trust gap worse, not better.

---

**Creative direction:**

All paid creative pulls from organic content that passed the 24–48h traction gate. The format is not designed; it is selected. If a phone-video post from a "Verified Voice" generates 80 shares in 48 hours, that post becomes the retargeting creative — untouched, with the voice's original framing, not redone by an agency.

The creative direction principle: authentic production beats polished production. Rough footage of a real person naming a real cost in their own words will outperform a designed carousel ad in this context. The opposition is running slick creative; your retargeting ad should look like nothing the audience has seen in the opposition's feed.

**Sample creative frame (warm retargeting):**
> [Name], a [profession] in [city], spent two years asking what this vote would mean for [specific local impact]. Here's what they found. [CTA: Read and sign.]

This frame: names a real person, names a real location, makes a specific claim, and leads to the investigation rather than to a donation page. The investigation page has the petition. Trust before ask.

---

**The 24–48h organic traction gate:**

Do not boost any post until it has demonstrated genuine organic signal: saves, shares, and comment-thread depth. Raw likes do not count — they are cheap and do not predict conversion. A post with 200 likes and 5 shares has weak signal. A post with 40 likes and 35 shares has strong signal. Boost the second one.

**Frequency cap:** Maximum 5 impressions per person per 2-week period. Beyond 5 impressions, conversion rates flatten and then go negative. With a 2-week creative refresh cycle, this means new creative every fortnight — not new campaigns, just new source post selected from the most recent organic winners.

**Audience definition:**
- Primary: email list custom audience (8,000 subscribers uploaded to Meta)
- Secondary: past website visitors (30-day window)
- Do NOT use: cold audiences, lookalikes, interest-based targeting. These are Tier 4 cold-paid approaches and are refused at this asymmetry level.

**Do not bid on the NGO's own brand-name keywords.** Not applicable here (this is petition/donation, not search capture), but worth noting for any future search campaign — Blake, Nosko & Tadelis's eBay field experiment found weak or zero incremental lift from brand-keyword bidding. Traffic arrives organically anyway.

**If board insists on broader paid spend:** present the Lewis & Rao (2015, QJE) finding that most digital ad campaigns cannot be statistically distinguished from zero lift, cite the prior €4k poor-conversion result, and require a geo-holdout lift test result before any budget increase above the €1,800 warm-retargeting allocation.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template: Template 1 (Geo-holdout) for warm retargeting spend. Template 5 (Organic-source attribution) for all organic channels.**

---

### Lift test — warm retargeting geo-holdout

**Hypothesis:** Warm Meta retargeting (email list custom audience) drives incremental petition sign-ups and donations on top of the organic baseline in the test region, at a cost per incremental sign-up below €6.

**Design:**
- **Test region:** Budapest and surrounding urban area (Hungary — largest single addressable sub-market, where volunteer infrastructure is already present)
- **Holdout region:** Krakow and surrounding urban area (Poland — comparable urban profile, also has volunteer meetup presence, so organic baseline is measurable)
- **Test condition:** Warm retargeting ads run only in Hungary on email list custom audience. No paid in Poland.
- **Organic baseline:** Maintain identical email cadence, identical social posting, and identical volunteer meetup cadence in both regions. The only difference is the warm retargeting ad in Hungary.
- **Duration:** 4 weeks (aligns with Weeks 3–6 of the campaign)
- **Metric:** UTM-tagged petition sign-ups and donation conversions, pulled from the petition platform's backend — not Meta-reported attributed conversions.
- **Measurement method:** Difference-in-differences. (Hungary sign-ups post-campaign − Hungary baseline) − (Poland sign-ups post-campaign − Poland baseline). Hungary baseline is Weeks 1–2 conversion rate before ads run. Poland is the control for trend.

**Decision threshold:**
- **Continue paid retargeting:** if incremental Hungary lift is at least 1.5× the cost of the warm retargeting spend (approximately €450/month) in sign-up equivalents (at a €6/sign-up target, need at least 112 incremental sign-ups attributable to the test).
- **Reallocate to organic:** if lift is positive but below the threshold, or statistically indistinguishable from zero. Redirect the €450/month to journalism partnership production costs or coalition partner coordination.
- **Stop paid entirely:** if lift is negative or zero AND the organic channels are outperforming by conversion rate. In that case, the board's 40% paid allocation is reallocated to organic infrastructure.

**Caveat:** This is a small-budget test. The confidence intervals will be wide. A result close to threshold should be interpreted as "no strong evidence of lift" rather than "confirmed lift." The burden of proof is on demonstrating incremental value, not on failing to prove it.

---

### Organic-source attribution — Template 5 (all organic channels)

**UTM scheme:**
- `utm_source=email-list&utm_medium=organic&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`
- `utm_source=instagram&utm_medium=organic&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`
- `utm_source=facebook-groups&utm_medium=organic&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`
- `utm_source=journalism-partner&utm_medium=earned&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`
- `utm_source=verified-voice-[initials]&utm_medium=organic&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`
- `utm_source=coalition-[org-slug]&utm_medium=partner&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`
- `utm_source=paid-retarget&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign=policy-vote-2026`

**Ranking metric:** Conversion rate per unique visitor (petition sign-ups / unique clicks), not absolute conversion volume. A newsletter sending 200 visitors with 40 conversions (20%) beats a viral Instagram post sending 5,000 visitors with 100 conversions (2%).

**30-day decision rule (applied at end of Week 4 and Week 8):**
- Top channel by conversion rate → double weekly effort allocation
- Any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate → drop or deprioritize unless it serves a secondary purpose (SEO, credibility, coalition relationship)
- If overall conversion rate across all channels falls below 0.5% → the bottleneck has shifted; re-run the MMF gate

**Caveat:** This is directional, not causal. Multi-touch attribution is underspecified. Someone who first saw the campaign via a "Verified Voice" post and later converted via the email newsletter will be attributed to email. Treat rankings as effort-allocation guides, not proof of lift.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track these

| Metric | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Petition sign-ups per week (by UTM source) | The campaign's primary outcome metric. Segmented by source to drive allocation decisions |
| Donations received per week (by UTM source) | Secondary outcome; validates that trust converts to financial commitment |
| Incremental sign-ups from geo-holdout test | The only metric that proves the warm retargeting spend is working |
| Per-channel conversion rate (sign-ups / unique visitors) | Drives the Template 5 weekly reallocation decision |
| Content saves and shares (not likes) | The organic traction gate signal; the only social metric worth watching before a boost decision |
| Volunteer recruits via coordinator network | Measures the mobilization layer; each volunteer recruited represents peer-to-peer reach no ad can replicate |
| Earned-media placements that name a specific person or document | Quality filter on press coverage; a mention of a named subject with a verifiable claim is worth 10× a generic "NGO says" mention |
| Email list open rate trend | Leading indicator of list health; a falling open rate signals trust erosion or deliverability problems |
| Verified Voice post conversion rates (UTM-tagged) | Identifies which non-NGO messenger categories convert best; informs next wave of recruitment |

### Ignore these

| Metric | Why it is misleading |
|---|---|
| Facebook/Instagram impressions | Reach is not trust. A million impressions from an NGO in a greenwashing-skeptical market is zero persuasion |
| CPM and platform-reported attributed ROAS | Platforms attribute conversions to themselves whether or not the ad caused the action (Lewis & Rao, QJE 2015). Attributed ROAS overstates incremental ROAS by 2–10× in meta-analyses |
| Total follower count | Follower count measures the past, not the campaign. An account with 2,400 followers and 20% share-to-like ratio outperforms one with 50,000 followers and 0.2% ratio |
| Raw likes on any post | Likes are cheap, often bot-inflated, and do not predict conversions or shares. They are the worst predictor of actual campaign traction |
| Email list size (without open rate segmentation) | An 8,000-person list at 12% open rate is 960 engaged humans. Report the 960, not the 8,000 |
| Page-level engagement rate (without content-type segmentation) | Instagram's 0.50% organic average means nothing for this campaign; a named-person video post at 4% and a generic graphic at 0.3% require different actions, and the average hides both |
| Number of petition sign-ups without source attribution | Signing is the output; knowing which channel drove the signing is the insight. Unsigned sources are noise |

---

**Sector failure-mode standing warning (Rider 3):**

The most common way NGO organic-first campaigns underperform: drift into donor-speak. An annual-report register in an Instagram caption. A policy-frame email subject line instead of a person's name. Organizational outputs ("we reached 2,400 people this month") instead of named outcomes ("Marta in Poznan signed last week because she couldn't insure her house against flooding after last summer"). Check every piece of content against this standard before publishing: if the first paragraph contains an acronym, an output metric, or the words "as an organization," rewrite it.
